title: CECS 460 -- Hardware/Software Co-Design and Design Flow
learning_objectives: Distinguish concrete, measurable requirements from vague goals and explain why the difference matters for partitioning
Apply the start-in-software heuristic to decide when a function should move to hardware or an existing accelerator
Explain why tape-out is the point of no return and how that economic reality shapes hardware/software partitioning decisions
Identify common co-design pitfalls (vague requirements, interface mismatches, version skew) and propose mitigations
Compare simple board-measurement modeling to industry co-simulation tools and articulate what risks each can catch
Evaluate firmware framework trade-offs between abstraction level, automation, and low-level control

hardware.wiring: No external hardware required for this chapter. The ESP32 DevKit-C is used as the software/hardware measurement platform in the lab (Lab 11).

reference_code.label: Lab 11 -- Hardware/Software Co-Design on the ESP32
reference_code.description: Measures execution time and estimated energy for a software-only AES-128 implementation vs. the ESP32 hardware AES engine. Reports results over MQTT for classroom comparison.

reference_code.label: Lab 11 -- AES Benchmark Firmware (classroom-integrated)
reference_code.description: Runs SW and HW AES-128 benchmarks automatically on boot, publishes results to MQTT for classroom grading, then prompts for typed reflection answer.

steps.title: 11.1 -- Requirements and Specification
steps.subtitle: The foundation of every partitioning decision

content_blocks.text: Before deciding what runs in hardware and what runs in software, you must know exactly what the system must do. A requirement specifies a measurable target: a number, a unit, and a pass/fail threshold. Without concrete requirements, partitioning decisions are guesswork.

content_blocks.heading: Concrete vs. vague requirements
rows: "End-to-end latency <= 200 ms from sensor to cloud"
"OTA firmware update completes within 90 s over Wi-Fi"
"AES-128 encryption on every transmitted packet, key rotated every 24 h"

content_blocks.text: A good specification is concrete (measurable targets with units), stable enough to avoid constant churn, and shared -- hardware, firmware, and test teams all work from the same document. Vague requirements like "fast" or "low power" cannot be verified and lead to teams making incompatible assumptions.

question.text: A product manager writes: "The device should have good battery life." Why is this requirement unacceptable as written?
choices: It does not specify the battery chemistry or cell count
It cannot be measured, tested, or used to make a hardware/software partitioning decision
Battery life is a manufacturing concern, not a design concern
It should be phrased as a non-functional requirement instead
question.explanation: "Good battery life" has no units, no threshold, and no test condition. The team cannot determine whether a deep-sleep current of 50 usA is acceptable or not, so they cannot decide whether to implement a ULP wakeup circuit in hardware or handle it in firmware. A concrete version: "Battery life >= 1 year at one sensor reading per minute, CR2032 cell."

content_blocks.text: Partitioning is the heart of co-design: for every function in the system, you decide whether it runs as code on a CPU or is implemented in dedicated hardware (custom block or existing accelerator). The practical rule: start in software unless you have a strong reason not to. Software is easier to change, debug, and reuse. Only move to hardware when measurement shows a specific operation dominates CPU time or energy.

content_blocks.heading: ESP32 hardware accelerators vs. software alternatives
rows: High packet rate or tight energy budget
CPU copies byte-by-byte in a loop
CPU wakes on timer, reads sensor, sleeps
ULP co-processor samples while CPU sleeps

content_blocks.text: Over-optimistic accelerator trap: Moving a function to hardware does not automatically make it faster end-to-end. If the time to move data into the accelerator (buffer copies, interrupt setup, DMA descriptors) exceeds the compute savings, you gain nothing. Always measure the full path, not just the compute kernel.

question.text: A firmware profiler shows that software AES-128 consumes 280 uss per call. The device encrypts one packet every 500 ms. Should this function be moved to the ESP32 hardware AES engine?
choices: Yes -- any function that takes more than 100 uss should always be in hardware
No -- 280 uss is only 0.056% of a 500 ms interval, so CPU headroom is not a problem
Yes -- hardware AES is always more energy-efficient regardless of call frequency
No -- the ESP32 hardware AES engine only supports AES-256
question.explanation: 280 uss / 500,000 uss = 0.056% CPU utilization. The software path is not a bottleneck at this call rate. The start-in-software rule says you only move to hardware when a specific operation demonstrably dominates CPU time or energy -- 0.056% does not qualify. If the packet rate increased to one every 5 ms (5.6% CPU), offloading would become worth evaluating.

content_blocks.text: Tape-out is the moment a chip design is sent to a semiconductor foundry to generate photomasks and begin wafer fabrication. Any bug in the RTL is replicated across every die on every wafer. Fixing a hardware bug after tape-out requires a respin: new masks, new wafer runs, months of schedule delay, and millions of dollars. This is why tape-out is called the point of no return.

content_blocks.heading: Cost to fix a bug: hardware vs. firmware
rows: Push OTA update to all devices
Firmware avoids broken block; uses alternate path
New mask set + new wafer run at foundry

content_blocks.text: Partitioning guideline: Functionality that changes frequently (protocol stacks, UI logic, business rules, OTA update sequences) should stay in firmware even when a hardware implementation is possible. Functions that are extremely performance-critical, energy-critical, or safety-critical (crypto engines, DRAM controllers, safety shutdown logic) justify hardware -- but only after thorough specification and pre-silicon verification.

question.text: A startup's SoC has just taped out. An engineer discovers a bug in the hardware AES block that causes incorrect output for 256-bit keys. The team estimates a firmware workaround adds 0.10% CPU overhead. What is the correct course of action?
choices: Immediately order a respin to fix the AES block
Ship the workaround in firmware -- 0.10% CPU overhead is negligible and avoids respin cost and delay
Disable the AES block entirely and remove encryption from the product
Delay the product until a new foundry run is complete, regardless of cost
question.explanation: 0.10% CPU overhead from a firmware workaround is essentially free. A respin to fix one hardware block would cost millions of dollars and months of delay for a negligible performance difference. This is exactly the scenario the "keep changeable logic in software" guideline is designed for: firmware can patch the problem at near-zero cost, while hardware cannot patch itself after tape-out.

steps.title: 11.3 -- Modeling and Simulation
steps.subtitle: De-risking architectural decisions before writing RTL

content_blocks.text: Before committing hardware resources to RTL, teams build models at increasing levels of fidelity. The goal is to catch architectural mistakes early, when they are cheap to fix. In CECS 460, the practical modeling approach is pseudocode + board measurements on the ESP32: implement the algorithm in C, measure execution time and current draw, and use the numbers to decide whether a hardware block is justified.

content_blocks.heading: Modeling fidelity spectrum
rows: Pseudocode + C on real board; timer/current measurements
SW performance bottlenecks, energy budget feasibility
Bus contention, DMA latency, cache coherency, interrupt timing at interfaces
Functional correctness of hardware logic, register-level behavior
Algorithm accuracy + HW timing together; floating-point vs fixed-point errors

content_blocks.text: CECS 460 approach: You will prototype the target computation on the ESP32, measure timing with esp_timer_get_time(), and optionally measure current with a multimeter or USB power meter. These numbers answer the key question: does software meet the requirements, or is hardware acceleration needed? This is the same first step industry teams take before authorizing RTL development.

question.text: A team implements a FIR filter in C on an ESP32 prototype and measures 3 ms per 256-sample window. Their product requirement is 100 ms per window. What does this measurement tell them?
choices: The software implementation is too slow -- a custom DSP hardware block is required
The software implementation meets the budget with 97 ms of headroom -- no hardware block is needed yet
The measurement is meaningless because it was taken on a prototype, not the production SoC
They must use SystemC TLM to validate this result before any conclusion can be drawn
question.explanation: 3 ms is well within the 100 ms budget -- the software path meets requirements with 97 ms of headroom. The start-in-software rule says: do not add hardware complexity until measurement shows software cannot meet the target. This prototype measurement is exactly the kind of early modeling CECS 460 uses to make evidence-based partitioning decisions.

content_blocks.text: Once partitioning is decided, the hardware team writes RTL and the firmware team writes drivers -- in parallel. The critical link between them is the hardware/firmware interface: a shared specification of register addresses, bit fields, byte order, reset state, interrupt lines, and handshake timing. If this document is vague or out of date, integration will fail.

content_blocks.heading: Common co-design pitfalls and mitigations
rows: Hardware and firmware teams made incompatible assumptions
Freeze a concrete spec before RTL starts
Driver writes wrong byte order, reset state, or timing
Shared register-map document reviewed and signed off by both teams
HW speedup is cancelled by data-movement overhead
Model the full path including DMA setup and interrupt cost
Bugs impossible to localize after tape-out
Add status registers, event counters, and trace support to RTL
Firmware built against old register map; HW is v2
Version-controlled interface spec; CI that checks consistency

content_blocks.text: Version skew is the most common silent killer in co-design: the hardware team updates a register address or bit field, the firmware team is not notified, and the driver silently writes to the wrong address. The fix is a version-controlled, machine-readable register description file (e.g., CMSIS SVD or IP-XACT) that both RTL and firmware header generation consume from a single source of truth.

question.text: After integrating with a newly delivered hardware AES block, the firmware driver produces corrupted output. Investigation reveals the hardware team used big-endian key byte order; the firmware team assumed little-endian. Which co-design pitfall does this represent?
choices: Over-optimistic accelerator design
Mismatched interface assumptions at the hardware/firmware boundary
Version skew between RTL and firmware header files
Insufficient debug hooks in the hardware block
question.explanation: Both teams made different assumptions about the same interface detail -- byte order of the key register -- because there was no shared, reviewed interface specification. This is the classic "mismatched interface assumptions" pitfall. The mitigation is a formal hardware/firmware interface document specifying byte order, reset values, valid input ranges, and timing, reviewed and approved by both teams before RTL is frozen.

content_blocks.text: Firmware frameworks exist on a spectrum from high-level (Arduino) to mid-level (ESP-IDF + FreeRTOS) to bare-metal register access. Higher abstraction means faster development and fewer low-level bugs; lower abstraction means precise control over timing, power, and peripheral state. The right choice depends on whether the target routine has strict energy or determinism requirements.

content_blocks.heading: Firmware framework trade-offs on the ESP32
rows: Clock init, Wi-Fi driver, peripheral setup, loop() structure
Register-level control, deterministic timing, low-overhead ISRs
Rapid prototyping, classroom labs, non-critical tasks
RTOS scheduling, partition table, NVS, most drivers
Fine-grained peripheral bit control; still has framework overhead
Production firmware with task isolation and OTA
Portability, safety from HAL; much longer dev time
ISR inner loops, ultra-low-power routines, custom peripheral timing

content_blocks.text: You do not have to pick one level for the entire firmware. A common pattern: use ESP-IDF for the application layer (Wi-Fi, MQTT, OTA), and drop to direct register access only for the one or two routines where timing or energy demands it. The goal is to isolate bare-metal code to the smallest possible footprint so the rest of the codebase remains maintainable.

question.text: A team uses Arduino-ESP32 for their product's main application loop. They discover the Arduino ADC wrapper adds ~12 uss of overhead per sample, but their control loop needs samples every 10 uss. What is the correct response?
choices: Switch the entire firmware to bare-metal register access to reduce overhead everywhere
Drop to direct register access only for the ADC sampling routine; keep Arduino for the rest
Reduce the control loop rate to 100 Hz to fit within the Arduino wrapper's timing
Use ESP-IDF instead of Arduino -- ESP-IDF has no ADC overhead
question.explanation: The overhead problem is isolated to one routine. Rewriting the entire firmware in bare-metal to fix one function is far more expensive than dropping to register-level access for just the ADC sampling ISR while keeping the higher-level Arduino or ESP-IDF framework for Wi-Fi, MQTT, and application logic. This is exactly the "isolate bare-metal to the smallest footprint" pattern.

steps.title: Lab 11 — Your AES Benchmark Results
steps.subtitle: Live hardware telemetry from your ESP32

content_blocks.text: Your ESP32 is running the AES-128 benchmark right now. It measures how long software-only AES takes versus the hardware AES engine, then auto-submits the results via MQTT. The data below updates live as your device reports.

The firmware runs 1000 blocks through both the software AES (mbedTLS, pure C) and the hardware AES engine (ESP32 crypto accelerator). The speedup factor tells you how much faster the dedicated hardware is for this specific workload.
If the data below says Waiting, check that your ESP32 is powered on, connected to the CECS Wi-Fi, and that the Serial Monitor shows [MQTT] Connected. The benchmark runs automatically on boot.

question.text: Your ESP32 benchmark results (auto-submitted by firmware — no typing needed):
question.explanation: The firmware auto-submits your benchmark telemetry. Full credit is awarded if sw_us and hw_us are in plausible ranges and hw_us < sw_us (hardware is faster than software).

steps.title: Lab 11 — Reflection: When Does Hardware Win?
steps.subtitle: Use your measured data to reason about the co-design tradeoff

content_blocks.text: You now have real measurements: your ESP32's software AES time and hardware AES time. The question is not whether hardware is faster (it obviously is), but at what workload the speedup matters enough to justify using the accelerator — and what costs the benchmark did not measure.

Think about this: if your software AES takes 280 µs per block and you encrypt one packet every 500 ms, that's only 0.056% CPU. The hardware engine is 100× faster, but 0.056% is already negligible. At what packet rate does CPU usage become a real concern?

content_blocks.heading: Overheads NOT captured by the benchmark:
items: Key schedule setup cost (esp_aes_setkey called before each encrypt)
DMA buffer allocation and alignment requirements
Interrupt round-trip latency from HW engine completion to CPU
Per-packet buffer copy cost (plaintext → aligned input buffer → ciphertext)
RTOS context switch overhead if AES runs in a dedicated task

question.text: Based on your measured speedup, at what packet rate (packets/second) would using the hardware AES engine become justified for a battery-powered IoT device? Show your reasoning using your actual measured sw_us and hw_us values. Then name ONE overhead the benchmark does NOT capture that would matter in production firmware.
question.placeholder:
Example: My SW AES = 280 µs, HW AES = 3 µs. At X packets/sec, software uses Y% CPU because...

One overhead not captured: ...
question.hint: Use your actual measured values from the previous step. Show the calculation: sw_us × packets_per_sec / 1,000,000 = CPU fraction.

questions.text:
A product manager gives your team these two requirements for a new ESP32-based device:

  (A) "The device must transmit a sensor reading within 350 ms of sampling."
  (B) "The device should have a long battery life."

(1) Classify each as concrete/measurable or vague. Justify each in one sentence.
(2) Rewrite requirement B as a concrete, measurable requirement. Include explicit units and a pass/fail threshold.
(3) Explain in two sentences how a vague requirement leads to a bad hardware/software partitioning decision.

Your software AES-128 implementation takes {value} uss per 128-bit block on the ESP32. The device encrypts exactly one packet every 200 ms.

(1) What percentage of CPU time does this AES task consume? Show your calculation.
(2) Based on the start-in-software heuristic, should you move this function to the ESP32 hardware AES engine? Justify your answer using your calculated percentage.
(3) At what approximate per-packet encryption rate (packets/second) would offloading to hardware start to become worth seriously evaluating? Explain your reasoning.

A startup is preparing to tape out a custom SoC. Three weeks before tape-out, a co-simulation run reveals a bug in the hardware SHA-256 block -- it produces incorrect digests for messages longer than 512 bits. A firmware workaround using a software SHA-256 library adds 0.08% CPU overhead.

(1) Define 'tape-out' and explain in two sentences why it is called the 'point of no return.'
(2) Should the team proceed with tape-out and ship the firmware workaround, or delay to fix the RTL? Justify your answer quantitatively.
(3) Name one type of product functionality that should always remain in firmware (not hardware) specifically to protect against expensive respins. Explain why.

A team is using ESP-IDF + FreeRTOS for their product firmware. Profiling shows the ESP-IDF ADC driver adds 15 uss of overhead per sample, but their closed-loop control task requires one ADC sample every 8 uss.

(1) Which firmware framework approach (Arduino, ESP-IDF, or bare-metal) should be used for the ADC sampling routine, and why?
(2) Name one thing that framework automates for the team in other parts of the firmware, and one thing they give up compared to the higher abstraction level.
(3) Describe one co-design integration pitfall (from Chapter 11) that this team should watch for as they mix abstraction levels in the same firmware.

Your ESP32 will automatically run the AES-128 benchmark and submit your results. When complete, the answer field will contain: [hw:sw_us=X hw_us=Y speedup=Z blocks=1000]

Do NOT type anything here -- the firmware submits this automatically. If you see 'q_lab1 OK: YES' in the serial monitor, your result has been received.

Reflection (type in Serial Monitor and press Enter): Based on your measured speedup, at what packet rate (packets/second) would using the hardware AES engine become justified for a battery-powered IoT device? Show your reasoning using your actual measured sw_us and hw_us values. Then name ONE overhead the benchmark does NOT capture that would matter in production firmware.
